summary: Q1 2026 total revenue was $22.39B (+16% YoY), with net income of $477M (+68M YoY). Automotive revenue grew 16% to $16.23B, driven by a 10% increase in cash deliveries and favorable sales mix. Energy generation and storage revenue fell 12% to $2.41B due to lower deployments. Total gross margin improved to 21.1% (vs 16.3% in Q1 2025). Liquidity remains strong with $44.74B in cash and short-term investments, up $684M from year-end 2025. Operating cash flow was $3.94B, while capital expenditures rose to $2.49B. 2026 capex guidance exceeds $25B, focused on AI, compute infrastructure, and manufacturing expansion. Risks include geopolitical conflicts, trade/tariff policy, supply chain constraints, and cyclical automotive demand. Capital allocation prioritizes vertical integration, AI/robotics R&D, and infrastructure scaling.

In 2025, TSLA reported $94.83B in total revenue (down $2.86B YoY) and $3.79B in net income (down $3.30B YoY). Automotive revenue fell 10% to $69.53B, driven by an 8% decrease in deliveries and lower average selling prices. Energy generation and storage revenue grew 27% to $12.77B, with 46.7 GWh deployed. Gross margin for total automotive was 17.8% (vs 18.4% in 2024), while energy segment margin improved to 29.8% (vs 26.2%). R&D expenses rose 41% to $6.41B, representing 7% of revenue. Liquidity remains strong with $44.06B in cash and investments, up $7.50B. Operating cash flow was $14.75B, while capital expenditures decreased to $8.53B. Guidance for 2026 capital expenditures is in excess of $20B, focused on AI, compute infrastructure, and manufacturing expansion. Risks include trade/fiscal policy uncertainty, tariffs, potential loss of EV tax credits, and cyclical automotive demand. Capital allocation prioritizes high-value investments in autonomy, robotics, and vertical integration.

Revenue/Profit: Q3 2025 revenue was $28.10B (+12% YoY), with net income of $1.37B (-$800M YoY). 9M 2025 revenue was $69.93B (-3% YoY) with net income of $2.95B (-$2.01B YoY). Automotive gross margin declined to 17.0% in Q3 2025 from 20.1% in Q3 2024, driven by lower fixed cost absorption, tariffs, and sales mix. Energy storage revenue grew 44% in Q3, with gross margin improving to 31.4%. Guidance: 2025 capital expenditures expected at $9.00B. Liquidity: $41.65B in cash, cash equivalents, and investments as of Q3 2025, up $5.08B from year-end 2024. 9M 2025 operating cash flow was $10.93B. Risks: Uncertain trade/fiscal policy, tariffs, inflationary pressures, interest rate fluctuations, and competitive market entry. Capital Allocation: Focused on AI, robotics (Optimus), autonomy (robotaxi/Cybercab), vertical integration, and infrastructure expansion.

For H1 2025, total revenue was $41.83B (down $4.97B YoY) and net income was $1.58B (down $1.21B YoY). Automotive revenue fell 18% to $30.63B, driven by 95,000 fewer Model 3/Y deliveries and lower ASPs. Energy generation/storage revenue grew 19% to $5.52B. Total gross margin declined to 16.8% from 17.7%. R&D expenses rose 35% to $2.99B, reflecting AI and product roadmap investments. Liquidity remains strong with $36.78B in cash and investments and $5.00B in unused credit. Operating cash flow increased to $4.70B, while capital expenditures decreased to $3.89B. Guidance expects 2025 capital expenditures to exceed $9.00B. Risks include trade/fiscal policy uncertainty, tariffs, inflationary pressures, interest rate volatility, and competitive intensity. Capital allocation focuses on AI, autonomy (Robotaxi), vertical integration, and infrastructure expansion.

Q1 2025 total revenue was $19.34B (down $1.97B YoY) and net income was $409M (down $981M YoY). Automotive revenue fell 20% to $13.97B, driven by a 51,000-unit decrease in Model 3/Y deliveries due to factory changeovers and lower ASPs. Energy generation/storage revenue grew 67% to $2.73B with 10.4 GWh deployed. Automotive gross margin compressed to 16.2% (from 18.5%), while energy segment margin expanded to 28.8% (from 24.6%). Liquidity remains strong with $37.00B in cash/investments and $5.00B in unused credit. Operating cash flow rose to $2.16B (up $1.91B YoY), while Q1 capital expenditures decreased to $1.49B. 2025 Capex guidance exceeds $10.00B. Risks include trade policy/tariffs, cyclical automotive demand, interest rate sensitivity, and supply chain volatility. Capital allocation focuses on AI, autonomy (robotaxi), battery technology, and global infrastructure expansion.

In 2024, TSLA reported $97.69B in revenue (+1% YoY) and $7.09B net income (-$7.91B YoY, impacted by 2023 tax valuation allowance release). Automotive revenue fell 6% to $77.07B due to lower average selling prices and a 22,000-unit decline in Model 3/Y deliveries, partially offset by Cybertruck growth. Energy storage revenue surged 67% to $10.09B on 31.4 GWh deployed. Total gross margin compressed to 17.9% (from 18.2%). Liquidity remains strong with $36.56B in cash and investments (+ $7.47B). Operating cash flow was $14.92B (+ $1.67B). Capital expenditures rose to $11.34B (from $8.90B) and are guided to exceed $11B annually through 2027. Risks include cyclical automotive demand, interest rate sensitivity, production ramp delays (e.g., new Model Y), and supply chain/labor constraints. Capital allocation prioritizes AI, autonomy, vertical integration, and infrastructure expansion.

Q1 2026 revenue was $12.25B (+16% YoY), driven by membership growth, price increases, and advertising. Operating income rose 18% to $3.96B, with a 32.3% margin (+0.6% YoY). Net income surged 83% to $5.28B, significantly boosted by a $2.8B WBD transaction termination fee. Cost of revenues increased 12% to $5.89B, primarily due to higher content amortization. Liquidity remains strong with $12.3B in cash and investments. Capital allocation includes $1.3B in Q1 2026 share repurchases, with $6.8B remaining under authorization. Material cash requirements include $24.1B in content obligations and $17.9B in debt. Management expects existing liquidity and cash flows to meet obligations for the next 12 months and beyond. Risks include foreign exchange volatility, potential price changes, and reliance on content investment strategies.

In 2025, Netflix reported total revenues of $45.18B, a 16% increase YoY, driven by membership growth, price increases, and advertising. Operating income rose 28% to $13.33B, with operating margin expanding to 29.5% from 26.7%. Net income was $10.98B, up 26%. Cost of revenues increased 11% to $23.28B, primarily due to higher content amortization and non-income tax assessments in Brazil. Liquidity remains strong with $9.07B in cash and investments; however, the company faces significant capital requirements, including $24.04B in content obligations and a $42.2B bridge facility commitment for the pending WBD acquisition. During 2025, the company repurchased $9.1B of common stock, with $8.0B remaining under current authorizations. Management expects existing cash and financing facilities to meet obligations for the next 12 months and beyond.

Q3 2025 revenue grew 17% YoY to $11.51B, driven by membership growth, price increases, and advertising. Operating income rose 12% to $3.25B, though operating margin contracted 1.4 percentage points to 28.2% due to rising content and marketing costs. A $619M non-income tax assessment in Brazil impacted Q3 cost of revenues. Liquidity remains strong with $9.33B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation focuses on content investment and share repurchases; $7.0B was used to repurchase 6.77M shares in the first nine months of 2025, with $10.1B remaining under authorization. Total contractual obligations include $20.94B for content and $18.39B for debt. Management expects existing cash and financing to meet needs for the next 12 months and beyond.

Q2 2025 revenue grew 16% YoY to $11.08B, driven by membership growth, pricing, and advertising. Operating income rose 45% to $3.77B, with operating margin expanding 6.9 percentage points to 34.1%. Net income increased 46% to $3.13B. Cost of revenues rose 3% to $5.33B, primarily due to content amortization. Liquidity remains strong with $8.39B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation includes $5.2B in stock repurchases during H1 2025, with $12.0B remaining under authorization. Debt decreased to $14.45B, with $690M in principal/interest due in the next 12 months. Significant cash requirements include $20.97B in total content obligations. Risks include foreign exchange volatility, competitive actions, and potential regulatory changes. Management expects cash flows from operations and existing financing to meet needs for the next 12 months and beyond.

Q1 2025 revenue grew 13% YoY to $10.54B, driven by membership growth and pricing. Operating income rose 27% to $3.35B, with margins expanding 3.6 percentage points to 31.7% as revenue growth outpaced cost increases. Content amortization remains the primary cost driver, rising $152M. Liquidity remains strong with $8.38B in cash and investments, despite $3.5B spent on share repurchases and $800M in debt repayment during the quarter. $13.6B remains available for buybacks. Capital allocation prioritizes global content investment, particularly original programming, which requires significant upfront cash. Contractual obligations include $21.79B for content and $19.24B for debt. Management expects existing cash and financing to meet needs for the next 12+ months. Risks include foreign exchange volatility, competitive actions, and potential regulatory changes. The company discontinued reporting membership metrics to focus on revenue and operating margin.

In 2024, Netflix reported total revenue of $39.0B, a 16% increase YoY, driven by a 15% rise in average paying memberships and price increases, despite unfavorable foreign exchange impacts. Operating income grew 50% to $10.4B, with operating margins expanding from 21% to 27% due to revenue growth outpacing cost increases. Paid net membership additions reached 41.35M, a 40% increase. Cost of revenues rose 7% to $21.0B, primarily due to a $1.1B increase in content amortization. Liquidity remains strong with $9.6B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation focuses on content investment, with $23.2B in total content obligations, and share repurchases; the board authorized an additional $15B for buybacks in Dec 2024, with $17.1B remaining available. Risks include foreign exchange volatility, particularly in Argentina, and potential inability to secure future financing on acceptable terms. The company expects cash flows from operations and existing facilities to meet needs for the next 12 months and beyond.

Q1 2026 net sales grew 17% YoY to $181.5B, driven by AWS (28% growth), North America (12%), and International (19%). Operating income rose to $23.9B from $18.4B in Q1 2025. Drivers include increased unit sales, advertising, and AWS usage, offset by higher infrastructure and fulfillment costs. Q2 2026 guidance projects net sales of $194B-$199B (16-19% growth) and operating income of $20B-$24B. Liquidity remains strong with $143.1B in cash and marketable securities as of March 31, 2026. Capital allocation is heavily focused on technology infrastructure and fulfillment capacity, with Q1 2026 cash capital expenditures of $43.2B. Significant strategic investments include $15B in OpenAI series C preferred stock, with a $35B commitment pending. Risks include macroeconomic volatility, foreign exchange fluctuations, regulatory/tax changes, and high dependency on infrastructure spending for AI/ML initiatives.

Amazon's 2025 net sales grew 12% to $716.9B, driven by AWS (20% growth), North America (10%), and International (13%). Operating income rose to $80.0B from $68.6B, despite $2.5B in FTC settlement charges and $2.7B in severance costs. AWS remains the primary profit engine ($45.6B operating income). Capital expenditures surged to $128.3B (vs $77.7B in 2024), primarily for technology infrastructure to support AI and AWS growth, causing free cash flow to decline to $11.2B from $38.2B. Liquidity remains strong with $123.0B in cash and marketable securities. The 2025 Tax Act significantly reduced cash taxes paid to $8.3B (vs $12.3B in 2024). Q1 2026 guidance projects net sales of $173.5B–$178.5B (11–15% growth) and operating income of $16.5B–$21.5B, factoring in $1B higher costs for Project Kuiper and international pricing investments. Key risks include FX volatility, AI/infrastructure investment scaling, regulatory/legal proceedings, and global economic conditions.

Revenue growth was 13% in Q3 2025 ($180.2B) and 12% for the nine months ($503.5B). Drivers include increased unit sales, third-party seller services, advertising, and AWS usage. AWS sales grew 20% in Q3 2025. Operating income was $17.4B in Q3 2025, impacted by a $2.5B FTC settlement and $1.8B in severance costs. Q4 2025 guidance projects net sales of $206B–$213B (10%–13% growth) and operating income of $21B–$26B. Margins are pressured by rising technology/infrastructure spending (up 30% in Q3 2025) and fulfillment costs. Liquidity remains strong with $94.2B in cash and marketable securities as of September 30, 2025. Operating cash flow for the trailing twelve months reached $130.7B. Capital allocation is heavily weighted toward technology infrastructure, with cash capital expenditures rising to $89.9B for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, up from $51.6B in the prior year. Risks include macroeconomic volatility, foreign exchange fluctuations, regulatory/tax changes, and potential inventory write-downs (1% allowance change equals ~$435M cost of sales impact).

Revenue drivers include increased unit sales, third-party seller services, advertising, and AWS usage. Q2 2025 consolidated net sales grew 13% to $167.7B. Operating income rose to $19.2B (Q2 2024: $14.7B), driven by North America and International retail efficiencies and AWS growth, despite higher infrastructure spending. Guidance for Q3 2025 expects net sales of $174.0B–$179.5B (10–13% growth) and operating income of $15.5B–$20.5B. Liquidity remains strong with $93.2B in cash and marketable securities as of June 30, 2025. Capital allocation focuses on technology infrastructure (AWS growth) and fulfillment network capacity; cash capital expenditures were $55.6B for H1 2025. Risks include macroeconomic volatility, foreign exchange fluctuations, regulatory/tax changes, and inventory valuation sensitivity (1% allowance change impacts cost of sales by ~$430M). The 2025 Tax Act is expected to reduce U.S. cash taxes via accelerated depreciation and R&D expensing.

Q1 2025 net sales grew 9% YoY to $155.7B, with AWS up 17%, North America up 8%, and International up 5%. Operating income rose to $18.4B from $15.3B in Q1 2024. Drivers include increased unit sales, advertising, and AWS usage, partially offset by higher fulfillment/shipping costs and technology infrastructure investments. Q2 2025 guidance projects net sales of $159B–$164B (7%–11% growth) and operating income of $13B–$17.5B. Liquidity remains strong with $94.6B in cash/marketable securities as of March 31, 2025. Capital allocation is heavily weighted toward technology infrastructure, with Q1 2025 cash capital expenditures of $24.3B (vs $13.9B in Q1 2024), expected to increase further in 2025. Risks include macroeconomic volatility, foreign exchange fluctuations, regulatory/tax uncertainties, and inventory valuation sensitivity (1% allowance change impacts cost of sales by ~$380M). Free cash flow for the trailing twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was $25.9B, down from $50.1B in the prior period, primarily due to higher property and equipment purchases.

Amazon's 2024 consolidated net sales grew 11% to $637.96B, driven by AWS (19% growth), North America (10%), and International (9%). Operating income rose to $68.59B (2023: $36.9B). Revenue drivers include unit sales, advertising, and subscription services. Operating income growth was supported by fulfillment efficiencies and AWS performance. Capital allocation focused on technology infrastructure, with cash capital expenditures rising to $77.7B (2023: $48.1B), expected to increase in 2025. Liquidity remains strong with $101.2B in cash and marketable securities. Risks include foreign exchange volatility (2024 impact: -$2.3B net sales), macroeconomic conditions, inflation, and regulatory/tax uncertainties. Q1 2025 guidance projects net sales of $151.0B–$155.5B (5–9% growth) and operating income of $14.0B–$18.0B, factoring in a $2.1B unfavorable foreign exchange impact.

Revenue increased 18% to $82.9B for the quarter, driven by a 29% rise in Microsoft Cloud revenue ($54.5B). Azure and other cloud services grew 40%, while Microsoft 365 commercial cloud revenue grew 19%. More Personal Computing revenue declined 1%. Operating income rose 20% to $38.4B. Gross margin percentage decreased due to AI infrastructure investments and product usage, despite efficiency gains. Operating expenses increased 9% to $17.7B, reflecting R&D and AI talent investments. Liquidity remains strong with $78.3B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation included $13.3B in share repurchases and $20.3B in declared dividends for the nine months ended March 31, 2026. Risks include intense competition, geopolitical/macroeconomic factors, supply chain dependencies for AI hardware, and a $28.9B IRS tax dispute regarding intercompany transfer pricing.

Q2 FY26 revenue grew 17% to $81.3B, driven by Intelligent Cloud (up 29%) and Productivity/Business Processes (up 16%). Microsoft Cloud revenue rose 26% to $51.5B; Azure/other cloud services grew 39%. Operating income increased 21% to $38.3B. Gross margin percentage decreased slightly due to AI infrastructure investments and product usage, despite efficiency gains. Operating expenses rose 5% to $17.0B, reflecting R&D for AI talent/compute and gaming impairment charges. Net income was $38.5B, boosted by $7.6B in net gains from OpenAI investments. Liquidity remains strong with $89.5B in cash/short-term investments. Capital allocation included $9.9B in share repurchases and $13.5B in dividends (six-month total). Risks include intense competition, geopolitical/macroeconomic volatility, supply chain dependencies for AI/datacenter components, and a $28.9B IRS tax dispute regarding intercompany transfer pricing.

Q1 FY2026 revenue grew 18% to $77.7B, with operating income up 24% to $38.0B. Revenue drivers: Microsoft Cloud (+26% to $49.1B), Azure (+40%), and Microsoft 365 Commercial (+17%). Gross margin was 69%, though Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage decreased to 68% due to AI infrastructure scaling and usage, partially offset by efficiency gains. Operating expenses rose 5% to $15.7B, driven by AI/cloud engineering and talent. Net income was $27.7B, negatively impacted by $3.1B (net of tax) in losses from OpenAI investments. Liquidity remains strong with $102.0B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation: $4.0B in share repurchases and $6.8B in dividends. Risks include intense competition, geopolitical/macroeconomic factors, supply chain volatility for datacenter/device components, and a $28.9B IRS tax dispute regarding transfer pricing.

FY2025 revenue grew 15% to $281.7B, with operating income up 17% to $128.5B. Revenue drivers included Microsoft Cloud (+23% to $168.9B), Azure (+34%), and Dynamics 365 (+19%). Gross margin was $193.9B (+13%), though Microsoft Cloud margin percentage declined to 69% due to AI infrastructure scaling. Operating expenses rose 6% to $65.4B, driven by AI/cloud engineering and Activision Blizzard. Liquidity remains strong with $94.6B in cash/investments. Capital allocation included $13.0B in share repurchases and $24.7B in dividends. Risks include intense competition, geopolitical/macroeconomic volatility, supply chain dependencies for AI hardware (GPUs), and a $28.9B IRS tax dispute. Guidance notes continued heavy investment in AI/cloud infrastructure and potential cost volatility from trade policies.

For the quarter ended March 31, 2025, revenue grew 13% to $70.1B, with operating income up 16% to $32.0B. Drivers: Microsoft Cloud revenue rose 20% to $42.4B; Azure and other cloud services grew 33% (16 points from AI). Productivity and Business Processes revenue increased 10%, and More Personal Computing rose 6%. Gross margin percentage decreased to 69% for Microsoft Cloud due to scaling AI infrastructure. Operating expenses rose 2% to $14.9B, driven by cloud and AI engineering. Liquidity remains strong with $79.6B in cash and short-term investments. Capital allocation: $9.8B in share repurchases and $18.5B in dividends declared for the nine months ended March 31, 2025. Risks include geopolitical/macroeconomic factors, supply chain volatility for AI infrastructure (GPUs/energy), and a $28.9B IRS tax dispute regarding transfer pricing. Guidance: Capital expenditures are expected to increase to support cloud and AI growth.

Revenue grew 12% to $69.6B (Q2) and 14% to $135.2B (6M). Key drivers: Microsoft Cloud (+21% to $40.9B), Azure (+31%), and Microsoft 365 Commercial Cloud (+16%). Operating income rose 17% (Q2) and 15% (6M). Margins: Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage decreased to 70% due to AI infrastructure scaling. Operating expenses rose 5% (Q2) and 8% (6M) on cloud engineering and Activision Blizzard integration. Liquidity: Cash/investments at $71.6B. Capital allocation: $6.3B share repurchases and $12.3B dividends (6M). Risks: High competitive intensity, geopolitical/macro factors, and supply chain dependencies for AI/datacenter components. Guidance: Capital expenditures expected to increase to support cloud/AI growth. IRS tax dispute involves $28.9B plus penalties/interest.

Q1 2026 net revenue was $1.3B (vs $1.9B in Q1 2025), with transaction revenue of $755.8M and subscription/services revenue of $583.5M. Net loss was $394.1M (vs $65.6M income in 2025), and adjusted EBITDA was $303.3M (vs $929.9M). Drivers include a 50% decline in trading volume to $202B and a 15% drop in MTUs to 8.2M, reflecting softer market conditions. Assets on platform fell 10% to $294B. Operating expenses were impacted by a 48% increase in technology and development costs. Liquidity remains strong with $10.4B in cash and marketable investments. A restructuring plan announced May 5, 2026, aims to lower Q2 2026 tech/development and G&A expenses. Capital allocation includes a $4B repurchase program ($2.1B remaining) and $7.3B in long-term debt, with $1.3B due within 12 months.

In 2025, Coinbase reported $6.9B net revenue ($4.1B transaction, $2.8B subscription/services) vs $6.3B in 2024. Net income was $1.3B (down from $2.6B) and adjusted EBITDA was $2.8B (down from $3.3B). Revenue drivers include derivative expansion (Deribit acquisition), USDC adoption, and staking. Trading volume rose 3% to $1.22T, though assets on platform fell 7% to $376B due to asset price declines. Liquidity remains strong with $11.6B in cash/marketable investments. Risks include interest rate sensitivity (89 bps decline in 2025), regulatory uncertainty, and potential volatility in crypto asset prices impacting investment holdings. Operating expenses rose significantly: sales/marketing increased 62% to $1.06B, and G&A rose 25% to $1.62B, partly due to a $345M data theft incident. Capital allocation includes a $2B repurchase program ($1.2B remaining) and $7.3B in long-term debt. Guidance for Q1 2026 expects tech/development and G&A expenses to align with Q4 2025 levels, with sales/marketing flat or lower.

Revenue drivers: Q3 2025 net revenue $1.8B (vs $1.1B in 2024). Transaction revenue $1.0B (up 83% YoY) driven by 74% increase in consumer trading volume and Deribit acquisition. Subscription/services revenue $746.7M (up 34% YoY) driven by USDC rewards and higher average balances. Trends: MTUs 9.3M (up 19% YoY); Assets on Platform $516B (up 95% YoY). Guidance: Q4 2025 expenses (Tech/Dev, G&A) expected to increase due to full-quarter Deribit/Echo impact and headcount growth. Risks: Interest rate declines may impact subscription/services revenue; crypto market volatility; regulatory compliance; data theft incident costs ($355.4M YTD). Liquidity: $8.7B in cash/cash equivalents plus $3.7B in USDC. Capital Allocation: $1.5B in 2029 and $1.5B in 2032 convertible notes issued in Aug 2025; $687.6M used for business combinations (Deribit) YTD; $487.7M increase in crypto investment purchases.

For Q2 2025, Coinbase reported $1.4B net revenue and $1.4B net income. Six-month net revenue was $3.4B with $1.5B net income. Revenue drivers include transaction fees (Q2: $764.3M) and subscription/services (Q2: $655.8M). Q2 Adjusted EBITDA was $512.1M. Trends show a 6% increase in MTUs to 8.7M and 63% growth in Assets on Platform to $425B. Guidance: Q3 2025 expenses for technology/development and G&A are expected to rise due to headcount growth; sales/marketing will remain in line with Q2. Risks include interest rate sensitivity, regulatory/policy changes, and potential volatility in crypto asset prices. Liquidity remains strong with $7.5B in cash/equivalents and $2.2B in USDC as of June 30, 2025. Capital allocation focuses on infrastructure, product innovation (derivatives, USDC integration), and maintaining regulatory compliance, with $4.3B in long-term debt outstanding.

Q1 2025 net revenue was $2.0B (vs $1.6B in Q1 2024), with $1.3B from transactions and $0.7B from subscriptions/services. Net income fell to $65.6M from $1.2B, while adjusted EBITDA was $0.9B (vs $1.0B). Revenue drivers include a 26% increase in total trading volume ($393B) and 21% growth in MTUs (9.7M). Subscription growth (37%) was led by a 51% rise in stablecoin revenue. Risks include potential interest rate declines impacting subscription revenue and volatility in crypto asset prices affecting investment valuations. Q2 2025 guidance expects lower tech/development and G&A expenses due to reduced variable support costs and seasonal payroll taxes, with modest growth in sales/marketing for USDC rewards and brand spend. Liquidity remains strong with $8.1B in cash/equivalents and $2.2B in USDC. Capital allocation focuses on operational flexibility, regulatory compliance, and long-term investment in crypto assets, with $4.3B in long-term debt outstanding.

In 2024, Coinbase reported $6.3B net revenue ($4.0B transaction, $2.3B subscription/services) vs $2.9B in 2023. Net income was $2.6B (vs $0.1B) and Adjusted EBITDA was $3.3B (vs $1.0B). Drivers: 148% increase in trading volume ($1.16T) and 112% growth in assets on platform ($404B). Revenue growth was fueled by institutional adoption, derivatives, and USDC rewards. Guidance for Q1 2025 expects modest growth in tech/development, G&A, and sales/marketing expenses due to headcount and variable costs. Liquidity remains strong with $8.5B in cash/equivalents and $1.2B in USDC. Capital allocation includes a $1.0B share repurchase program authorized in Oct 2024 (no shares repurchased as of Dec 31, 2024) and $4.3B in long-term debt. Risks include interest rate volatility impacting subscription revenue, regulatory uncertainty, and market-dependent trading volumes.
